artifacts.content:
Hi Hannah,

I’m hiding in the Willow Creek office between Zooms and thought I’d actually write instead of sending another chaotic text thread.

First, Dad: how is he really doing this week? I keep thinking about Sacred Heart after his stroke in 2011 and then the ladder fall in 2021, and how every time we think we’re through the worst, something else pops up. I’ve blocked off the first weekend in April to come down to Eugene so you’re not carrying all of Maggie’s oncology appointments alone. Can we sync around her next round at the cancer center and who’s taking which Sacred Heart runs for Bob’s cardiology follow‑ups?

Work is its usual “nothing… and everything” at once. I’m still holding that long-running exec team at the regional hospital, plus the hybrid SaaS product leadership team downtown. On top of that I’ve got a supervision case from Cascadia Health Systems rattling around in my head again – remember when I did that informal, pro bono supervision practicum there from September 2016 to May 2017? Five internal coaches, about 25 hours of group supervision and 15 hours individually, all because of that ugly wrongful‑termination lawsuit in October 2016 about systemic bullying in the ICU. Three of them ended up on stress‑related medical leave. I can still picture that OD office in Portland and the whiteboard full of “safe escalation routes” we drew.

Some days it feels like my whole career is just walking into other people’s crises and trying to help them see the system around them. Then I come home and it’s spelling lists, Jake’s science project, and Ellie’s soccer carpools.

Speaking of, can you send me Oliver’s and Lucy’s spring break dates? The kids keep asking when they’ll see their cousins.

Love,
M

Hi Laura,

I’ve just made myself a second cup of coffee and wanted to say a proper thank‑you after the ethics masterclass last month.

The timing could not have been more on‑the‑nose. In February, one of my supervisees – an internal team coach at the health tech company where my husband Daniel is a senior product manager – disclosed that he’d cornered her after a retro and “just casually” asked what had come up in a confidential team coaching session about a product VP’s drinking problem and those repeated Monday‑morning hangovers. It hit every single conflict‑of‑interest and boundary nerve I have. We did the right things in the moment (she didn’t share anything, we re‑contracted, I named the dual relationship), but it left a residue.

Your 6‑hour online ethics in supervision masterclass on Zoom in April really helped me sort through what belongs in supervision, what belongs in my own therapy (Dr. Mills at Providence is getting an earful), and what needs to be a very boring, grown‑up conversation with Daniel about lines he cannot cross as long as I’m supervising anyone in that building downtown.

On a lighter note, I’m trying to keep Fridays as “thinking/admin” days again – only one client, then supervision notes, CPD planning, and maybe actually watering the tomatoes before they wilt. I’m also sketching ideas for that group supervision cohort for team coaches in healthcare/tech I mentioned; your piece on power and proximity is going to be required reading.

Hope you’re getting at least glimpses of spring wherever you are.

Warmly,
Melissa

Working CPD / supervision history – rough notes while I procrastinate on tidying the office.

Supervision‑related training that actually shaped my practice:

– 2019 Advanced Diploma in Coaching Supervision with Coach Supervision Academy (London, blended): 150+ hours plus 40 hours of supervised supervision practice. That year still feels like a turning point. Alongside the diploma I did those extra 12 hours of one‑to‑one reflective supervision with Dr. Karen Lewis at the Portland Jungian Clinic to process the vicarious trauma from supervising the internal coaching cohort at Cascadia Health Systems after the senior physician on their executive team died by suicide in June 2019. Those sessions were as much about my own nervous system as they were about “learning.”

– 2020 Spring: 8‑week supervision skills booster (~24 hours) with Helena Morris through Pacific Northwest Coaching Collective – I signed up after my long‑term executive client at that Portland health tech startup started disclosing active opioid misuse and suicidal ideation in our March 2020 Zooms, right as the first COVID lockdowns hit. It gave me more language and courage to hold the boundary and insist on a referral.

Earlier stuff:

– There was that very early weekend “supervision” workshop I did back in 2014 – unaccredited, lots of buzzwords, not much depth. At the time I was just hungry for anything with the word “supervision” on it, but in hindsight it was pretty superficial and not really aligned with best practice. A useful lesson in being choosier.

Note: Between finishing the CSA diploma in Dec 2019 and the Helena course in 2020, I also started but quietly dropped out of a year‑long advanced team coaching supervision programme – I simply couldn’t carry it on top of client work and the kids.

Hi Janet,

I’m doing my annual “how did another year of supervision happen?” reflection and thought you’d appreciate a candid download.

Formally I’ve been supervising since January 2019, starting with the Advanced Diploma practicum, so we’re at about six years now. The hours add up – somewhere around 350–400 of formal supervision delivered – but they’ve come in waves. Remember that 30‑hour gap in my log from June through August 2020? That was when Abernethy Elementary shut down after the COVID outbreak in Ellie’s 5th‑grade class and Jake’s 2nd‑grade pod. I was suddenly home‑schooling long division and Oregon Trail projects instead of running group supervision.

The supervision‑of‑supervision with Alan Hughes from October 2021 to March 2022 was a lifesaver. Eighteen hours with that Exeter group, and we spent a good chunk of it on the Cascadia Health Systems case where my supervisee – an internal coach – was under an informal HR investigation for allegedly breaching confidentiality. She’d mentioned a VP’s panic attacks and Xanax use in a team coaching debrief in November 2021. Untangling my contracting there made me much braver about naming limits up front.

On the personal side, Daniel and I are mostly okay after the 2020 “surprise” credit card saga, the kids are thriving (Ellie is deep into soccer; Jake dismantled an old router yesterday), and I’m still guarding Fridays as thinking/admin days.

I’m also noticing a two‑year lull after the CSA diploma where I didn’t do much formal supervision‑specific CPD – I focused on business development and just keeping the wheels on through the pandemic. Not ideal, but honest.

If you have any thoughts on what would stretch me next, I’d love to hear them.

With gratitude,
Melissa

Event: Supervision / therapy support block – Cascadia + health‑tech cases

Date: 2020-04-16 (Thursday)
Time: 13:00–15:30
Location: Home office (Richmond) – Zoom / phone
Attendees: Me; 13:00–14:00 Helena (Pacific Northwest Coaching Collective); 14:15–15:00 Dr. Karen Mills (Providence Behavioral Health); 15:00–15:30 unscheduled decompression.

Notes:

– 13:00–14:00 – Supervision skills booster session with Dr. Helena Morris (week 3 of 8, ~24‑hour online course). Bring the long‑term executive client from the Portland health tech startup who disclosed active opioid misuse + suicidal ideation in March 2020 sessions, right as COVID first lockdowns started. Questions: what is firmly clinical/therapeutic vs. what remains coaching; how to hold the boundary without abandoning him.

– 14:15–15:00 – Telehealth with Dr. Karen Mills at Providence Behavioral Health (downtown Portland). Ongoing work around anxiety, insomnia, and the residual vicarious trauma from multiple healthcare‑related cases (Cascadia internal cohort after the senior physician’s suicide in June 2019; ICU bullying/wrongful‑termination supervision from 2016–2017; OHSU client from 2016 who disclosed opioids/suicidal ideation). Also check in on how the emerging pandemic + kids at home is re‑activating some of the old postpartum patterns.

– 15:00–15:30 – Walk around the block, no phone. Jot quick reflections when I get back: what belongs in supervision (Helena/Janet/Alan), what belongs in therapy (Mills), what belongs in practical boundary conversations with clients and Daniel.

Reminder: Ask Daniel to handle school pick‑up logistics if schools close early.

Personal CPD / business planning – Friday thinking time

Sitting in the Willow Creek office with lukewarm tea, trying to make sense of the next 3–5 years.

Supervision focus:
– I’ve been practicing formally as a coach supervisor since Jan 2019. Roughly 350–400 hours logged, plus the 18 hours of group supervision‑of‑supervision with Alan Hughes (University of Exeter) between October 2021 and March 2022, where we dug into three knotty cases – especially the Cascadia Health Systems supervisee who was under informal HR investigation for allegedly breaching confidentiality after talking about a VP’s panic attacks and Xanax use in a team coaching debrief in November 2021.

– Ethics side strengthened by Laura Kim’s 6‑hour online masterclass last April, catalysed by that February 2023 situation where my supervisee at Daniel’s health tech company told me he’d informally pressured her to share details from a confidential team coaching session about a product VP’s drinking problem and perpetual Monday‑morning hangovers. Still feel a little sick writing that.

Team coaching / supervision ambitions:
– I’d like to deepen specifically in team coaching supervision – maybe something like a shorter, high‑quality intensive rather than another year‑long programme. I started one in 2019 and quietly let it go when it became obvious that, on top of the Advanced Diploma, client work, and the kids, it was simply too much.

– Keep Fridays mostly client‑free: admin, reflection notes, newsletter drafting, and maybe one supervision group.

Also: remember the early, slightly embarrassing 2014 “supervision” weekend that was all buzzwords and very little substance. Good reminder to vet providers carefully.

Action: shortlist 2–3 CPD options for 2025 that are genuinely stretching, not just shiny.

Hi Karen,

I’m holed up in the downtown office between PSU marking and a supervision group and suddenly realised I haven’t properly updated you in ages.

Work first: team coaching has fully taken over my life in the best and worst ways. I still think of those three co‑facilitations we did together at OHSU in late 2015 – me just back from postpartum leave after Jake, trying not to cry in the elevator – as the real beginning. Officially I say I’ve been a team coach since 2016, but you and I know it started in those scrappy hospital conference rooms.

Since then it’s been a blur: the 14‑month executive team engagement at St. Andrew’s Regional in Eugene that kicked off three weeks after Dad’s heart attack in January 2016; the first paid team coaching contract with that mid‑sized SaaS firm downtown (the $18,000 one that let Daniel and me finally pay off the last $6,500 of my PSU loans in Spring 2017); and now this ongoing hybrid product leadership team that can’t decide if it’s one team or four.

On the supervision side, the Cascadia stories keep echoing. I still think about the informal, pro bono supervision practicum I did there from September 2016 to May 2017 – five internal coaches, 25 hours of group and 15 hours of individual supervision, all in the shadow of that wrongful‑termination lawsuit over ICU bullying. And then, later, the 2019 Advanced Diploma year with CSA and the extra 12 hours I did with Karen Lewis at the Portland Jungian Clinic to process the suicide of the senior physician on their executive team. It’s a lot of grief to carry in one nervous system.

Home is the usual chaos: Ellie’s soccer tournaments, Jake dissecting old electronics, Daniel trying not to live in Slack. I’m still guarding Fridays as “thinking/admin” days so I don’t slide back into 55‑hour weeks.

How are you, really? Any chance you’ll be in Portland this fall?

With love,
Melissa

From: melissa@cartwrightleadership.com
To: sarah.wilkes@example.com
Date: 2025-01-18
Subject: Draft bio + the “how long have you been coaching?” question

Hey Sarah,

You were right, trying to pin down “how long I’ve been practicing as a coach” is more tangled than it sounds.

In strictly paid terms, I usually say “about 13 years,” counting from early 2012 when I started taking formal external clients alongside my OD role at Cascadia Health Systems in Portland. But the roots go back further. During my MSc work at Oregon Health & Science University Hospital in 2009, I was doing pro bono coaching with charge nurses on the cardiac unit. One of them (I called her “Karen” in my research notes) ended up taking FMLA leave in November 2010 after a near‑miss medication error. Those sessions convinced me I wanted to keep doing this work long‑term.

By September 2011 I was picking up paying executive clients while still doing 40+ hour weeks at Cascadia. Part of the push was Dad’s minor stroke that October down in Eugene; I was driving to Sacred Heart Medical Center every other weekend for about six months, so I restructured toward 15–20 client hours I could flex around rehab visits.

Then there’s the even messier layer: the informal coaching I was doing with leaders at Cascadia in 2010–2011, and honestly with Hannah back in Bend during her divorce and custody mediation in fall 2008, when we spent endless evenings talking through options with her lawyer about the parenting plan for Oliver and Lucy.

So: for the website, maybe we keep it simple—“over 12 years of professional coaching experience”—and I can unpack the nuance in conversation.

M

metadata.subject: Draft bio + the “how long have you been coaching?” question

artifacts.content:
From: melissa@cartwrightleadership.com
To: janet.lewis@example.com
Date: 2025-02-03
Subject: Updating supervision CV – timeline sanity check

Hi Janet,

I’m refreshing my supervision CV for the EMCC renewal and realized my “years of experience” story is all over the place.

On the individual side, I’ve technically been coaching since late 2012, when my then‑manager in Organizational Development at Cascadia Health Systems, Karen Liu, shifted part of my workload into internal coaching after I came back from a short‑term stress leave in November 2012 for postpartum depression following Ellie’s birth. Those first 6–9 months were very part‑time while I was still in treatment at Providence Behavioral Health in Portland and on a phased return to work. Around the same time, in October 2012, Mark Ellison (our OD director at Cascadia) put me on a last‑chance performance plan after that conflict with the senior surgeon over an OD project and handed me three internal coaching clients as a kind of test. The work went well and essentially saved my job, and I’ve been coaching continuously since then.

I did scale back for about four months in Spring 2019 when Jake was hospitalized twice at Doernbecher Children’s Hospital in Portland after his Type 1 diabetes diagnosis. During that pause I only kept two long‑term executive clients at around 5 hours/month total so I could be at the hospital and learn his new care routine.

Does “just over 12 years of continuous coaching practice, with a brief reduced‑load period in 2019” sound accurate enough for EMCC?

Warmly,
Melissa

metadata.subject: Updating supervision CV – timeline sanity check

artifacts.content:
From: melissa@cartwrightleadership.com
To: daniel.cartwright@example.com
Cc: hannah.reynolds@example.com
Date: 2025-02-21
Subject: Coaching hours + family stuff – trying to make the puzzle work

Hi you two,

I just sketched out a more honest coaching timeline because all the “how long have you been doing this?” questions are making me rethink capacity for the next few years.

Team coaching really became my core in 2016. That was when I left the part‑time role at the community mental health clinic on SE Belmont after the difficult HR investigation into a colleague’s boundary violation with a client. Around the same time I did that pro bono engagement with the board of the addiction recovery nonprofit in Southeast Portland, where Hannah had just finished her intensive outpatient program for prescription painkillers. Those two experiences are why I now say I’ve been a team coach for about 9 years and why Cartwright Leadership & Team Coaching, LLC exists at all.

Between mid‑2014 and early 2015 I ramped up to about 25 coaching hours per month, taking on several short‑term, lower‑fee clients through that nonprofit career center in Southeast Portland so we could help cover Hannah’s custody mediation legal fees in Bend during the divorce from Eric. Looking back, that was my first big red flag around overwork.

On the supervision side, I’ve been practicing formally for just over 6 years, since my Advanced Diploma in Coaching Supervision practicum started in January 2019. I’d enrolled right after taking a late‑2018 leave of absence from my adjunct role at Portland State University because of stress‑related insomnia and panic attacks. Dr. Karen Mills at Providence Behavioral Health in downtown Portland named it as work‑related anxiety from juggling my executive coaching load, PSU teaching, and the kids’ schedules at Abernethy Elementary. In parallel, I was already doing informal peer supervision through the Portland ICF chapter in 2017, partly to process vicarious trauma from that long‑term OHSU executive client who was disclosing active opioid misuse and suicidal ideation. You’ll remember how much I leaned on you, Janet, and those sessions in your office near Good Samaritan Hospital.

All of this is a long way of saying: my “years of experience” have often been built on top of family crises and health scares. As I plan the next phase, I want us to be explicit about how much I can sustainably hold.

Love,
M

metadata.subject: Coaching hours + family stuff – trying to make the puzzle work

artifacts.content:
From: melissa@cartwrightleadership.com
To: sarah.mitchell@northsoundcoaching.com
Date: 2025-01-11 08:17 AM
Subject: Bio question – how long have you been coaching?

Hey Sarah,

I’m updating my bio for the systemic team coaching masterclass in Seattle and got stuck on the deceptively simple question: “How long have you been practicing as a coach?”

On paper, I usually say I’ve been practicing as a coach for about 13 years, counting from early 2012 when I started taking formal external clients while I was still in my MSc at Portland State. That’s when I logged my first paid coaching hours in January 2012, squeezing in evening executive coaching sessions at that tiny shared office on SW 5th Avenue in downtown Portland after full days at Cascadia Health Systems.

But if I’m honest, the roots go back further. I was doing a lot of informal coaching with leaders at Cascadia Health Systems in Portland, OR in 2010–2011, even though no one was calling it “coaching” yet. My then-manager Elena Morales only really named it after things went sideways when I came back from maternity leave with Eleanor “Ellie” Grace Cartwright in May 2014. I was missing a few early-morning sessions thanks to postpartum insomnia and childcare chaos, and she put me on a formal “development plan.” It was awful at the time, but it forced me to get very intentional about boundaries and what I was actually offering.

So depending on how you count, it’s either 13 years of formal practice or closer to 15 years of real coaching conversations.

Curious how you frame this in your own materials.

Warmly,
Melissa

metadata.subject: Bio question – how long have you been coaching?

artifacts.content:
From: melissa@cartwrightleadership.com
To: margaret.reynolds48@example.com
Date: 2016-09-03 09:42 PM
Subject: Remember when coaching became “real”?

Hi Mom,

I’ve been working on a money narrative exercise for my supervision group and it brought me right back to those scrappy years in Portland when coaching shifted from “interesting side thing” to “we actually rely on this to pay the bills.”

Depending on how you count it, I’ve been practicing as a coach for roughly 13–14 years now. I always mark January 2012 as the official start, because that’s when I logged my first paid coaching hours while still in my MSc program at Portland State University. Do you remember how frantic that winter felt? I’d finish class, bike over to our shared office on SW 5th Avenue in downtown Portland, and see evening executive coaching clients until 8:30 or 9:00 p.m.

I had to accelerate that transition because Daniel was laid off from his product manager role at a Portland health tech startup in March 2011. Overnight we went from “we’re fine” to staring at nearly $18,000 in credit card debt plus $650/month in minimum payments on my student loans. Taking on those evening clients at the SW 5th office was the only way to keep us afloat without moving back in with you and Dad.

It’s sobering and oddly grounding to remember that coaching, for me, has always sat at the intersection of purpose and very practical financial reality.

Love,
Melissa

Title: Supervision prep – my coaching origin story
Date: 2024-10-19
Author: Dr. Melissa Anne Cartwright
Location: Cartwright Leadership & Team Coaching, LLC – Willow Creek Lane office

Prompt: “Map the key inflection points in the development of your coaching identity, including financial and systemic pressures.”

• 2010–2011 – Cascadia Health Systems in Portland, OR. Officially “leadership development,” unofficially lots of informal coaching with leaders. No one called it coaching, but the conversations had all the hallmarks.

• March 2011 – Daniel Robert Cartwright laid off from his product manager role at a Portland health tech startup. We slide into nearly $18,000 in credit card debt + $650/month in student loan minimums. Money anxiety becomes a constant background hum.

• January 2012 – While in my MSc program at Portland State University, I log my first paid coaching hours. Evening executive coaching clients at the shared office on SW 5th Avenue in downtown Portland. This is the moment coaching becomes not just identity, but economic necessity. Depending how I count, this is the start of my 13–14 years as a “practicing coach.”

• May 2014 – Return from maternity leave with Eleanor “Ellie” Grace Cartwright. Postpartum insomnia + childcare unpredictability = missed early-morning sessions at Cascadia. My manager, Elena Morales, puts me on a development plan. Painful, shaming, AND clarifying. I begin to see that I’ve effectively been coaching for closer to 15 years if I include those early Cascadia conversations.

Working question for supervision group: How do I hold both narratives (13 years vs. 15 years) without collapsing into imposter stories or inflation?

metadata.title: Supervision prep – my coaching origin story
metadata.location: 742 Willow Creek Lane, Suite 300, Portland, OR 97204

artifacts.content:
From: melissa@cartwrightleadershipcoaching.com
To: hannah.reynolds@bendpedsclinic.org
Subject: Re: Mom’s treatment dates + your visit
Date: 2025-02-11

Hey Han,

Just caught up on your texts from Eugene and the new dates for Mom’s next round. I’ve blocked the March 5–7 window on my calendar so I can be down there with you and handle driving her to the oncology clinic.

You asked the other day how long I’ve actually been doing supervision because one of your colleagues was curious about “second-career pivots.” Counting from when I started my supervised practicum in January 2019, I’ve been practicing as a coach supervisor for roughly 6 years now. It still surprises me it’s been that long.

You’re one of the few people who remembers the wobble in the middle of that stretch. That 3‑month dip in Spring 2020, when everything hit at once—COVID, schools closing, and then the discovery of Daniel’s hidden $18,000 credit card balance—feels like a lifetime ago and also yesterday. When we started couples counseling with Dr. Laura Bennett at the Portland Relationship Center on SE Belmont, I knew I couldn’t keep pretending everything was fine at work. I temporarily reduced my supervision caseload from 10 clients to 3 just to manage the emotional fallout and the extra childcare.

I’m oddly grateful for that period now; it’s given me a lot more compassion when my supervisees hit their own “life happens” moments.

Call me tonight after the kids are in bed? I’ll be home from campus by 8:30.

Love,
M

Dr. Melissa Anne Cartwright
Cartwright Leadership & Team Coaching, LLC
742 Willow Creek Lane, Suite 300
Portland, OR 97204
